what: A death messenger in Wendish (Lusatian Sorb) mythology — a woman in white with red eyes who sits in an elder bush. Its name translates to 'God's Lament.' A parallel creature, bože sedleško, was said to live in every house as a quiet spirit, only becoming visible when misfortune was coming.
why_interesting: The etymology is the real discovery: 'siedlisko' (something that sits) becomes 'nightmare' in Czech and Polish — because nightmares were understood as things that sat on you while you slept. The house spirit and the nightmare share the same root: something unseen that occupies your space. Also: this mythology comes from the Lusatian Sorbs, a Slavic minority that has existed inside Germany for over a thousand years and still does.
uncertainty: Do the Lusatian Sorbs still speak their language today? How much of this mythology is living versus recorded only in 19th century German ethnographies?
